title: Arab Spring begins in Tunisia
summary: On 17 December 2010, in the interior Tunisian town of Sidi Bouzid, a 26-year-old street vendor named Mohamed Bouazizi set himself on fire outside the governorate offices after a municipal inspector confiscated his fruit cart and slapped him. His act, filmed on mobile phones and shared on Facebook, triggered protests that within a month toppled President Zine el-Abidine Ben Ali and touched off uprisings across the Arab world. Regimes fell in Egypt, Libya, and Yemen; civil wars followed in Libya, Syria, and Yemen; the political map of the region was redrawn. What began as a demand for dignity in a small town became the most consequential political upheaval in the Middle East and North Africa since decolonisation.

sections.heading: Background
sections.body:
In the winter of 2010 the Arab world was ruled almost entirely by ageing autocrats. Zine el-Abidine Ben Ali had presided over Tunisia since 1987; Hosni Mubarak had ruled Egypt since 1981; Muammar Gaddafi had controlled Libya since 1969; Ali Abdullah Saleh had led north Yemen and then a unified Yemen since 1978; Bashar al-Assad had inherited Syria from his father in 2000; the Al Saud, Al Khalifa, Al Thani, and Hashemite families ran the Gulf and Jordan. Behind various formal constitutions and periodic elections these were security states, sustained by extensive police and intelligence apparatuses, patronage networks, and, in most cases, hydrocarbon or aid-derived rents.

Beneath the surface, pressures were mounting. The region had one of the world's youngest populations: median age in Egypt and Tunisia was under 25. Youth unemployment was 30 per cent or more in several countries; university graduates could not find work commensurate with their qualifications. A generation had grown up with satellite television, from 1996, Al Jazeera in Arabic, and from the mid-2000s, with the internet, mobile phones, Facebook, and Twitter, which allowed political grievances and organising to circulate outside state media. The global 2008 financial crisis and the surge in food prices in 2010, driven partly by drought in Russia and Ukraine, sharpened economic pain across the Arab world.

Tunisia under Ben Ali had, by regional standards, achieved rising incomes, near-universal schooling, and a relatively secular legal framework. But growth was uneven, the interior lagged the coastal cities, and the president's extended family, especially his second wife Leila Trabelsi's kin, had grown notorious for corruption. A 2008 uprising in the phosphate-mining Gafsa region had been crushed. WikiLeaks cables released in late November 2010 documented American diplomats' contempt for the Ben Ali clique in language that reached Tunisian readers via Al Jazeera and social media.

Mohamed Bouazizi, born in 1984 in Sidi Bouzid, was a self-employed fruit and vegetable vendor supporting his mother and siblings. Like many young men in the interior he lacked the connections to secure a formal permit and, according to accounts collected by journalists, had been repeatedly harassed by municipal inspectors demanding bribes. On the morning of 17 December 2010, an inspector named Fedia Hamdi (later cleared by a court in 2011) confiscated his scales and produce and, according to his family, publicly slapped him. He went to the governorate seeking the return of his goods; refused a hearing, he doused himself in paint thinner in front of the building at around midday and set himself alight. He was rushed with severe burns to a hospital in Ben Arous.

sections.heading: What happened
sections.body:
Bouazizi's self-immolation on 17 December 2010 was, in itself, not unusual: Tunisia had seen several such acts of protest in preceding years. What followed was. Family, friends, and other unemployed young men gathered outside the Sidi Bouzid governorate on 18 December; police attacked the crowd; a lawyer filmed the confrontation on his mobile phone and uploaded it to Facebook, whence it was picked up by Al Jazeera Mubasher and broadcast across the region. Within days protests had spread to nearby towns, Menzel Bouzaiene, where a young protester was shot dead on 24 December, Regueb, Kasserine, and Thala.

As demonstrations reached the coastal cities, the Ben Ali regime alternated between concession and repression. On 28 December the president visited Bouazizi in hospital, a photograph of the encounter distributed by state media. On 4 January 2011 Bouazizi died of his burns. His funeral drew thousands. Trade unions, especially the powerful Union Generale Tunisienne du Travail (UGTT), joined the protests. On 12 January the army chief of staff, General Rachid Ammar, reportedly refused Ben Ali's order to fire on demonstrators in central Tunis. On 13 January Ben Ali announced he would not seek re-election in 2014; on 14 January, as tens of thousands gathered on Habib Bourguiba Avenue, he and his family flew to Jeddah, Saudi Arabia. Twenty-eight days after Bouazizi's self-immolation, the first Arab head of state had been forced from power by popular protest.

The example galvanised the region. On 25 January 2011, chosen deliberately as Egyptian 'Police Day', mass protests filled Tahrir Square in Cairo. Eighteen days of demonstrations, met by regime-linked thugs on camels and by internet blackouts, ended on 11 February when Vice President Omar Suleiman announced on state television that Hosni Mubarak had resigned and handed power to the military high command. In Bahrain, a Shia-majority country ruled by the Sunni Al Khalifa dynasty, protests centred on Pearl Roundabout in Manama beginning 14 February were crushed a month later after Saudi and Emirati troops crossed the King Fahd Causeway.

In Libya, protests beginning in Benghazi on 15-17 February 2011 escalated within days into an armed uprising. Gaddafi's forces threatened to reconquer Benghazi in mid-March; on 17 March the UN Security Council passed Resolution 1973 authorising 'all necessary measures' to protect civilians, and a NATO-led air campaign began on 19 March. After seven months of civil war Tripoli fell to rebel forces on 23 August. On 20 October 2011 Gaddafi was captured in a drainage culvert near his hometown of Sirte and killed by fighters. In Yemen, sustained protests, an assassination attempt against Ali Abdullah Saleh on 3 June 2011, and Gulf-brokered mediation led to his formal departure on 27 February 2012. In Syria, protests that began on 15 March 2011 in Deraa were met with lethal force from the outset by Bashar al-Assad's regime, spiralling by 2012 into a full civil war.

sections.heading: Aftermath & consequences
sections.body:
The trajectories of the Arab Spring diverged sharply. Tunisia held free elections in October 2011, adopted a new constitution in January 2014, and awarded the Nobel Peace Prize to its 'National Dialogue Quartet' of civil society organisations in 2015 for negotiating a peaceful transition. It remained, for a decade, the only Arab Spring country to have consolidated a democratic system, though President Kais Saied, elected in 2019, dissolved parliament in 2021 and rewrote the constitution in 2022 in moves widely characterised as an autocratic reversal.

Egypt held its first free presidential election in 2012, won by the Muslim Brotherhood's Mohamed Morsi. Deep polarisation followed. Massive protests on 30 June 2013 provided the pretext for a military coup led by Defence Minister Abdel Fattah el-Sisi on 3 July; Morsi was detained (and died in a Cairo court in 2019). The Rabaa massacre of 14 August 2013, in which security forces killed at least 800 pro-Morsi protesters in a single day, marked the definitive end of the democratic opening. Sisi has ruled since, with tens of thousands of political prisoners.

Libya after Gaddafi fragmented. A civil war from 2014 pitted rival governments in Tripoli and Benghazi against one another, drawing in Turkish, Emirati, Egyptian, Russian, and other foreign forces. Yemen's transition collapsed into war after 2014, when Houthi rebels seized Sanaa; a Saudi-led coalition intervened in March 2015. By 2020 the United Nations described Yemen as the world's worst humanitarian crisis, with widespread famine and cholera.

Syria's civil war became the most destructive of the twenty-first century's first two decades. By 2016 half of its pre-war population of some 22 million had been displaced; Russia intervened militarily on Assad's side in September 2015; Iran and Hezbollah backed the regime; Turkey, the Gulf, and, more ambivalently, the United States backed elements of the opposition. The Islamic State group emerged from the chaos, proclaiming a 'caliphate' across parts of Syria and Iraq in 2014. Assad regained control of most Syrian territory by 2020, but his regime unexpectedly collapsed in December 2024 under a lightning offensive by rebel forces led by Hayat Tahrir al-Sham.

More than a million people fled to Europe in the peak refugee year of 2015, most of them Syrians, contributing to the political rise of anti-immigrant parties across the continent, to the Brexit vote of 2016, and to bitter divisions inside the EU over asylum policy. The Gulf monarchies, having weathered the initial protests, entered a new phase of assertive foreign policy, especially under Saudi Arabia's Crown Prince Mohammed bin Salman from 2017.

sections.heading: Legacy
sections.body:
A decade on, the balance sheet of the Arab Spring is contested. For its participants, and for those chronicling it in real time, it was a moment of extraordinary hope: mass, largely peaceful mobilisation across a region long thought immune to democratic change. For its critics, and for many of its early enthusiasts by the 2020s, it produced war, exile, and, in most cases, harder authoritarianism. The Syrian, Libyan, and Yemeni civil wars alone have killed several hundred thousand people and displaced more than fifteen million.

Socially and technologically the uprisings marked a moment. They were the first political upheavals in which social media, especially Facebook, Twitter, and YouTube, played a demonstrable role in coordinating protest and circumventing state control of information. Al Jazeera Arabic acted as a de facto continental broadcaster. Regimes learned quickly: internet shutdowns, digital surveillance, and manipulation of social media became standard authoritarian tools.

Economically, most Arab Spring countries are worse off than in 2010. Tourism, foreign investment, and remittances collapsed in the affected states; oil-exporting Gulf economies also faced pressures from the 2014-2016 oil-price crash and the 2020 pandemic. The demographic and youth-employment pressures that drove the 2011 protests have not been resolved; if anything they have intensified.

Historians will continue to debate causation. Some emphasise economic stagnation and food prices; others the role of social media; others the timing of a generational shift; others the specifics of army loyalty in Tunisia and Egypt versus its fragmentation in Libya and Syria. What is beyond dispute is that on 17 December 2010, in a place unmarked on most maps, one man's despairing act became the trigger for a decade of regional transformation whose consequences, from the Islamic State to the European refugee politics of the late 2010s to the fall of Assad in 2024, are still being worked out. Sidi Bouzid's central square now bears Mohamed Bouazizi's name, and a modest monument depicts the cart the municipal inspector took away.

worldContext.text: Europe in December 2010 was in the grip of the euro-area sovereign debt crisis. Ireland had accepted an 85-billion-euro EU/IMF rescue on 28 November 2010; Portugal would follow in May 2011; the Greek programme entered its second and much larger phase. Britain's coalition government under David Cameron had begun a decade of austerity in June. In France, President Nicolas Sarkozy prepared for a 2012 re-election he would lose. WikiLeaks, its founder Julian Assange arrested in London on 7 December 2010, was mid-way through releasing US diplomatic cables that included coverage of Ben Ali's regime.

The United States under President Barack Obama had just seen the Democrats' 'shellacking' in the November 2010 midterms, which delivered the House to a Tea Party-inflected Republican majority. American troops had ended combat operations in Iraq in August 2010; the Afghan war continued at its peak, with US forces above 100,000. Obama would, in March 2011, authorise US participation in the NATO air campaign over Libya. In Mexico, Felipe Calderon's war on the drug cartels ground on with more than 15,000 killed in 2010 alone.

China had overtaken Japan as the world's second-largest economy in the second quarter of 2010. In October the Nobel Peace Prize was awarded, to Beijing's fury, to the imprisoned Chinese dissident Liu Xiaobo. On 23 November North Korea shelled the South Korean island of Yeonpyeong, killing four; regional tensions ran high. Japan approached a general political and economic reckoning that would culminate in the Fukushima earthquake, tsunami, and nuclear accident of 11 March 2011.

In Pakistan, the summer 2010 floods had submerged a fifth of the country, displacing 20 million people; the recovery was still under way. The war in Afghanistan continued at its highest intensity of the whole conflict. In India, the country was preparing to co-host the 2011 Cricket World Cup, but the Anna Hazare-led anti-corruption movement was gathering strength, would bring hundreds of thousands into the streets by mid-2011, and prefigured the political turn that brought Narendra Modi to power in 2014.

Sub-Saharan Africa's own dramatic political story of that winter was in Ivory Coast, where the incumbent Laurent Gbagbo refused to concede after losing the 28 November 2010 presidential election to Alassane Ouattara, triggering a brief civil war that ended with Gbagbo's arrest in April 2011. In Sudan the referendum on southern independence was scheduled for 9 January 2011 under the 2005 Comprehensive Peace Agreement; South Sudan would formally become an independent state on 9 July 2011. Nigerian elections looming in April 2011 would bring Goodluck Jonathan a full term.

figures.name: Mohamed Bouazizi
figures.role: Tunisian street vendor whose self-immolation on 17 December 2010 triggered the uprising; died 4 January 2011

figures.name: Zine el-Abidine Ben Ali
figures.role: President of Tunisia 1987-2011; fled to Saudi Arabia on 14 January 2011

figures.name: Hosni Mubarak
figures.role: President of Egypt 1981-2011; resigned 11 February 2011 after 18 days of protests centred on Tahrir Square

figures.name: Muammar Gaddafi
figures.role: Libyan ruler since the 1969 coup; overthrown and killed during the 2011 civil war

figures.name: Ali Abdullah Saleh
figures.role: President of Yemen 1990-2012 (of North Yemen from 1978); ceded power under Gulf-brokered deal

figures.name: Bashar al-Assad
figures.role: President of Syria since 2000; retained power through a long civil war until his regime fell in December 2024

images.caption: More than a million protesters fill Tahrir Square in central Cairo on 9 February 2011, two days before President Hosni Mubarak's resignation.
Protesters in Tahrir Square, Cairo, on 8 February 2011, during the eighteen-day Egyptian revolution that followed Tunisia's example.

sources.title: Remembering Mohamed Bouazizi: The man who sparked the Arab Spring
The Arab uprisings: The people want the fall of the regime
